title: 93.	DP with monotonic queue: Jump Game VI.
description: 93.	DP with monotonic queue: Jump Game VI.

approaches.name: Brute Force DP O(n*k)
approaches.summary: Use dynamic programming where dp[i] stores the maximum score to reach index i, computed by checking all k previous indices. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DP Solution for Jump Game VI
ladders.desc: Complete O(n*k) dynamic programming solution to find maximum score path with k-step jumps
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force DP solution for Jump Game VI. The intuition is that to reach index i, we must have come from one of the previous k indices (i-k to i-1), so we can compute dp[i] as nums[i] plus the maximum dp value from those previous positions. This creates a recurrence relation: dp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[i-k], dp[i-k+1], ..., dp[i-1]) for i > 0, with dp[0] = nums[0]. The algorithm initializes dp[0] = nums[0], then for each i from 1 to n-1, it checks all valid previous positions j where max(0, i-k) ≤ j < i, computing dp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[j]) over those j. The answer is dp[n-1]. Time complexity is O(n*k) because for each of n positions, we check up to k previous positions. Space complexity is O(n) for the dp array. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large n and k. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling the base case dp[0] correctly, 2) Forgetting to check that previous indices are valid (≥ 0), 3) Off-by-one errors in the range of j, 4) Not initializing dp array properly. This brute force approach serves as the foundation for understanding why we need optimization (which leads to the monotonic queue approach).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Maximum in Sliding Window of Previous DP Values
ladders.desc: Compute max(dp[j]) for j in [i-k, i-1] for each position i
ladders.explanation: This is the core computational bottleneck in the brute force approach. For each position i, we need to find the maximum dp value among the previous k positions (from i-k to i-1, bounded by 0). The intuition is that to compute dp[i], we need the best score we could have had when arriving at position i, which comes from the maximum of the reachable previous positions. The algorithm: For each i from 1 to n-1, initialize max_val = -inf, then iterate j from max(0, i-k) to i-1, updating max_val = max(max_val, dp[j]). Finally, dp[i] = nums[i] + max_val. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(k) per i, leading to O(n*k) overall. Space complexity is O(1) for the temporary max_val variable. This sub-routine directly feeds into the full problem by being the inner loop that computes each dp[i]. Common mistakes: 1) Starting j from i-k without checking if it's negative, 2) Including dp[i] in the max calculation (should only be previous positions), 3) Not resetting max_val for each i, 4) Using the wrong comparison operator. This repetitive max computation is what makes the brute force approach inefficient and motivates optimization techniques.

examples.trace:
i=4, k=2, valid j range: max(0,4-2)=2 to 3
Initialize max_val = -inf
j=2: dp[2]=-1, max_val=max(-inf,-1)=-1
j=3: dp[3]=4, max_val=max(-1,4)=4
Result: max_val=4

i=5, k=3, valid j range: max(0,5-3)=2 to 4
Initialize max_val = -inf
j=2: dp[2]=8, max_val=max(-inf,8)=8
j=3: dp[3]=14, max_val=max(8,14)=14
j=4: dp[4]=14, max_val=max(14,14)=14
Result: max_val=14

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence Relation for Maximum Path Score
ladders.desc: Formulating dp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[i-k...i-1]) with boundary conditions
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental recurrence relation that defines the problem. The intuition: At position i, your score consists of the value at that position (nums[i]) plus the best score you could have had from any of the previous k positions. This works because you can jump from any of those positions to i. The recurrence is: dp[0] = nums[0] (base case, starting position). For i ≥ 1: dp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[j]) for j in [max(0, i-k), i-1]. The max(0, i-k) ensures we don't go before the start of the array. This recurrence captures the optimal substructure property: the optimal solution to reach i depends on optimal solutions to reach previous positions. Time complexity analysis shows this leads to O(n*k) if implemented naively. Space complexity is O(n) for storing dp values. This core logic connects to the key sub-routine by specifying exactly what maximum needs to be computed. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting the base case dp[0], 2) Incorrectly defining the range of j (should be i-k to i-1, not i-k+1 to i), 3) Adding nums[i] outside the max instead of after, 4) Not handling the case when i-k < 0. Understanding this recurrence is crucial for recognizing why we need to optimize the max computation.

examples.trace:
First compute dp[0]=2
dp[1]=nums[1]+max(dp[0])=3+2=5
For dp[2]: valid j range: max(0,2-2)=0 to 1
max(dp[0],dp[1])=max(2,5)=5
dp[2]=nums[2]+5=1+5=6
Wait, correction: dp[2]=1+5=6, but let's trace properly:
dp[0]=2
dp[1]=3+max(dp[0])=3+2=5
dp[2]=1+max(dp[0],dp[1])=1+max(2,5)=1+5=6
Actually 6, but the example says 5. Let me fix: The output should be the dp value, not the max. So dp[2]=6. But the example is computing something else. Let me re-read: 'compute dp[2]' so output is dp[2]=6. But the example says 5. I need to correct this.
Actually, looking at the example format, it seems to be showing the max value from previous positions, not dp[2]. Let me create a proper example.
Better example: For nums=[2,3,1], k=2
dp[0]=2
dp[1]=3+max(dp[0])=3+2=5
dp[2]=1+max(dp[0],dp[1])=1+max(2,5)=1+5=6
So dp[2]=6

dp[0]=1
dp[1]=-2+max(dp[0])=-2+1=-1
For dp[2]: valid j range: max(0,2-1)=1 to 1
max(dp[1])=max(-1)=-1
dp[2]=3+(-1)=2
Wait, that's 2 not 4. I need to check my example.
Actually, with k=1, you can only come from immediately previous position.
So dp[2]=3+dp[1]=3+(-1)=2
But the example says 4. This is wrong. Let me fix the example.
Correct example: nums=[1,5,3], k=1
dp[0]=1
dp[1]=5+max(dp[0])=5+1=6
dp[2]=3+max(dp[1])=3+6=9
That's 9, not 4. I need to create a consistent example.
Let me use: nums=[1,2,3], k=1
dp[0]=1
dp[1]=2+1=3
dp[2]=3+3=6
Still not 4. I'll create a proper working example.
Example: nums=[1,-1,4], k=2
dp[0]=1
dp[1]=-1+max(dp[0])=-1+1=0
dp[2]=4+max(dp[0],dp[1])=4+max(1,0)=4+1=5
So dp[2]=5

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Array Traversal with Sliding Window
ladders.desc: Processing array elements sequentially while maintaining a window of relevant indices
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing an array from left to right while keeping track of a sliding window of indices. The intuition is that many array problems require examining elements in relation to their neighbors within a certain range. For this problem, we need to process each position i and consider the k positions before it. The algorithm: Initialize an array dp of size n. Set dp[0] = nums[0]. For i from 1 to n-1: determine the start index start = max(0, i-k). Then examine all indices j from start to i-1 to find the maximum dp[j]. Finally, dp[i] = nums[i] + that maximum. Time complexity is O(n*k) because the inner loop runs up to k times for each i. Space complexity is O(n) for the dp array. This connects to the core logic by implementing the recurrence relation through iterative traversal. Common mistakes: 1) Incorrect loop boundaries (off-by-one errors), 2) Not updating the window correctly as i advances, 3) Confusing the window of indices with the window of values, 4) Not handling the case when the window size is larger than available indices. This pattern of sliding window traversal appears in many DP problems where the current state depends on a fixed number of previous states.

examples.trace:
n=3, dp=[0,0,0]
Initialize dp[0]=nums[0]=2
i=1: start=max(0,1-2)=0, check j=0, max=dp[0]=2, dp[1]=nums[1]+2=1+2=3
i=2: start=max(0,2-2)=0, check j=0,1, max=max(dp[0],dp[1])=max(2,3)=3, dp[2]=nums[2]+3=3+3=6
Final dp=[2,3,6]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Maximum Over a Subarray
ladders.desc: Finding the maximum value in a contiguous subarray of an array
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation where given an array arr and indices l and r (inclusive), we find max(arr[l], arr[l+1], ..., arr[r]). The intuition is simple: iterate through the specified range and keep track of the largest value seen. The algorithm: Initialize max_val = -infinity (or a very small number). For index j from l to r: if arr[j] > max_val, set max_val = arr[j]. After the loop, max_val contains the maximum. Time complexity is O(r-l+1), linear in the size of the subarray. Space complexity is O(1). This operation connects to the building block as it's exactly what we need to do for each position i when computing dp[i] - we need the maximum dp value in the subarray from max(0,i-k) to i-1. Common mistakes: 1) Using the wrong initial value for max_val (should be smaller than any possible array value), 2) Incorrect loop boundaries (off-by-one), 3) Modifying the original array during the computation, 4) Forgetting to handle empty subarrays (when l > r). This basic operation appears in countless algorithms and is essential for understanding more complex patterns like sliding window maximum.

examples.trace:
Initialize max_val = -inf
j=1: arr[1]=1, max_val=max(-inf,1)=1
j=2: arr[2]=4, max_val=max(1,4)=4
j=3: arr[3]=2, max_val=max(4,2)=4
Result: 4

Initialize max_val = -inf
j=0: arr[0]=-5, max_val=max(-inf,-5)=-5
j=1: arr[1]=-3, max_val=max(-5,-3)=-3
j=2: arr[2]=-1, max_val=max(-3,-1)=-1
Result: -1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Iteration
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements by index and looping through arrays
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept: understanding how arrays store elements at indices (positions) and how to access them. An array arr of size n has indices 0 through n-1. The element at index i is accessed as arr[i]. Iteration means visiting each element in order, typically with a for loop: for i in range(n): access arr[i]. The intuition is that arrays provide random access - we can directly access any element if we know its index. This is crucial for DP because we need to store and retrieve intermediate results. Time complexity for accessing one element is O(1). Space complexity for the array is O(n). This foundation connects to the basic operation because to find the maximum in a subarray, we need to access each element in that range. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (arrays start at 0), 2) Accessing indices outside the array bounds (causing index errors), 3) Confusing the index with the value stored at that index, 4) Not understanding that array indices are integers. This concept is prerequisite for all array-based algorithms.

examples.trace:
arr[0] = 10
arr[1] = 20
arr[2] = 30
Loop: i=0 -> access arr[0]=10
i=1 -> access arr[1]=20
i=2 -> access arr[2]=30

examples.input: arr = [5,3,7,1], iterate and print indices and values
examples.trace:
Array has 4 elements (indices 0-3)
i=0: index 0, value arr[0]=5
i=1: index 1, value arr[1]=3
i=2: index 2, value arr[2]=7
i=3: index 3, value arr[3]=1

approaches.name: DP with Max-Heap O(n log k)
approaches.summary: Maintain a max-heap to efficiently find the maximum dp value among the last k indices for each step. This reduces the time complexity by avoiding linear scans over k elements.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game VI with Max-Heap DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with max-heap to find maximum score path with k-step jumps.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum score achievable by starting at index 0 and making jumps of at most k steps forward to reach the last index. The naive DP approach would be O(nk) by scanning all possible previous positions. The key optimization is using a max-heap to efficiently find the maximum dp value among the last k indices. For each position i, we need the maximum dp[j] where j is in [i-k, i-1]. A max-heap stores (dp_value, index) pairs. At each step, we pop elements from the heap until the top element's index is within the valid range (i-k to i-1). This ensures we always have the maximum valid dp value in O(log k) time. The dp recurrence is dp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[j] for j in [max(0, i-k), i-1]). We initialize dp[0] = nums[0] and the heap with (nums[0], 0). For each i from 1 to n-1, we clean the heap by removing outdated indices, then dp[i] = nums[i] + heap[0][0] if heap is not empty (otherwise -inf). We push (dp[i], i) to the heap. Finally, return dp[n-1]. Time complexity is O(n log k) since each index is pushed once and popped at most once. Space is O(n) for dp array and O(k) for heap. The intuition is that we maintain a sliding window maximum using a heap rather than linear scan, similar to priority queue solutions for sliding window problems but adapted for DP.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0]=1, heap=[(1,0)]. i=1: valid range j in [-1,0] -> [0,0]. Clean heap: top (1,0) is valid. dp[1]=-1+1=0, push (0,1), heap=[(1,0),(0,1)]. i=2: range j in [0,1]. Clean heap: both valid. Max dp=1 at index 0. dp[2]=-2+1=-1, push (-1,2), heap=[(1,0),(0,1),(-1,2)]. i=3: range j in [1,2]. Clean heap: remove (1,0) (index 0 < 1). Top (0,1) valid. dp[3]=4+0=4, push (4,3), heap=[(4,3),(0,1),(-1,2)]. i=4: range j in [2,3]. Clean heap: remove (0,1) (index 1 < 2). Top (4,3) valid. dp[4]=-7+4=-3, push (-3,4), heap=[(4,3),(-1,2),(-3,4)]. i=5: range j in [3,4]. Clean heap: all valid. Max dp=4 at index 3. dp[5]=3+4=7. Return dp[5]=7.

dp[0]=10, heap=[(10,0)]. i=1: range j in [-2,0] -> [0,0]. Clean heap: top (10,0) valid. dp[1]=-5+10=5, push (5,1), heap=[(10,0),(5,1)]. i=2: range j in [-1,1] -> [0,1]. Clean heap: both valid. Max dp=10. dp[2]=-2+10=8, push (8,2), heap=[(10,0),(8,2),(5,1)]. i=3: range j in [0,2]. Clean heap: all valid. Max dp=10. dp[3]=4+10=14, push (14,3), heap=[(14,3),(10,0),(8,2),(5,1)]. i=4: range j in [1,3]. Clean heap: remove (10,0) (index 0 < 1). Top (14,3) valid. dp[4]=0+14=14, push (14,4), heap=[(14,3),(14,4),(8,2),(5,1)]. i=5: range j in [2,4]. Clean heap: remove (5,1) (index 1 < 2). Top (14,3) valid. dp[5]=3+14=17. Return 17.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Max-Heap for Sliding Window Maximum
ladders.desc: Implement a max-heap that efficiently provides maximum value in the last k indices while removing outdated elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the optimization. We need a data structure that can quickly give the maximum dp value among the last k indices and remove elements that fall outside this sliding window. A max-heap (implemented as a priority queue with negative values for max) stores pairs (dp_value, index). At each step i, we need the maximum dp[j] where j is in [i-k, i-1]. The heap may contain elements with indices less than i-k (outdated). We repeatedly pop from the heap while the top element's index < i-k. This ensures the top element is always the maximum valid dp value. Each element is pushed once and popped at most once, giving amortized O(log n) per operation. The intuition is that we're maintaining a 'lazy deletion' heap: instead of actively removing outdated elements when the window slides, we remove them only when they become the top and are invalid. This is efficient because each element is processed only twice. In code, we use a heap that stores (-dp_value, index) to simulate max-heap in Python's min-heap. The subroutine takes current index i, k, and heap, and returns the maximum valid dp value or -inf if heap becomes empty. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called at each DP step to get max previous dp value.

examples.trace: Valid range for j: [i-k, i-1] = [1,2]. Heap is max-heap via negatives: actual values [10,5,8]. Clean heap: top is (-10,0) -> index 0 < 1, so pop. New top (-8,2) -> index 2 is in [1,2], valid. Return 8 (negative of -8).
Valid range j: [3,5]. Clean heap: top (-4,3) index 3 is in [3,5], valid. Return 4. No pops needed.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence with k-Step Constraint
ladders.desc: Formulate the dynamic programming recurrence relation for maximum score with jump limit k.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that the maximum score at index i (dp[i]) depends on the maximum score achievable from previous indices within k steps. Specifically, dp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[j]) for j in [max(0, i-k), i-1]. This means to reach index i, you must have come from some index j that is at most k steps behind. The score at i is the value at i plus the best score you could have had at j. This recurrence captures the optimal substructure property: the optimal solution to reach i uses an optimal solution to reach some previous j. The base case is dp[0] = nums[0] since you start at index 0. The answer is dp[n-1]. The naive implementation would compute max(dp[j]) by linear scan over up to k elements, giving O(nk) time. The optimization replaces this linear scan with a heap. The intuition is similar to other DP problems with window constraints (like climbing stairs with variable steps), but here we need the maximum, not sum. Connection to L1: The heap subroutine efficiently computes max(dp[j]) for this recurrence.

ladders.title: Building Block — Max-Heap Operations for Priority Queue
ladders.desc: Implement and use a max-heap (via min-heap with negation) to support push, pop, and peek maximum.
ladders.explanation: A max-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is greater than or equal to its children, allowing O(log n) insertion and O(log n) extraction of the maximum element. In Python, we typically use the heapq module which provides min-heap operations. To simulate a max-heap, we store negative values. For example, to push value v, we push -v. To pop max, we pop and return -value. To peek max, we look at -heap[0]. This building block is essential for efficiently maintaining the maximum dp value in a sliding window. Operations needed: heapify (O(n)), heappush (O(log n)), heappop (O(log n)), and heap[0] for peek (O(1)). We'll store pairs (negative_dp_value, index) to break ties by index if needed (though not strictly necessary). The intuition is that a heap gives us fast access to the maximum without sorting all elements each time. Connection to L2: This heap is used to compute max(dp[j]) in the DP recurrence.

examples.trace: Start empty. Push 5: push -5 -> heap[-5]. Push 3: push -3 -> heap[-5,-3]; heapify -> [-5,-3]. Push 8: push -8 -> heap[-5,-3,-8]; heapify -> [-8,-3,-5]. Peek max: -heap[0] = 8.
Heap represents values [10,3,7]. Pop: heappop returns -10, so max = -(-10)=10. Heap becomes [-7,-3]; heapify -> [-7,-3]. Peek new max: -(-7)=7.

testCases.input: Push 7, push 2, push 9, push 4, pop, pop
Push 1, push 2, push 3, pop, push 4, peek

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Traversal with Index Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array while calculating valid index ranges using k.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves looping through indices from 1 to n-1 and for each i, determining the range of previous indices j that are valid (within k steps back). Specifically, j must satisfy: max(0, i-k) ≤ j ≤ i-1. This ensures j is not negative and is at most k steps behind. In code, we use a for loop for i in range(1, n): start_j = max(0, i-k). Then j can be any integer from start_j to i-1 inclusive. This operation is basic but crucial for setting up the DP window. The intuition is that at each step, you can only jump from indices that are reachable within the jump constraint. Connection to L3: This traversal defines when heap elements become outdated (indices < start_j).

examples.input: n=5, k=2, iterate i from 1 to 4
examples.trace: For i=1: start_j = max(0,1-2)=0, end_j=i-1=0, so j in [0,0]. i=2: start_j=max(0,0)=0, end_j=1, so [0,1]. i=3: start_j=max(0,1)=1, end_j=2, so [1,2]. i=4: start_j=max(0,2)=2, end_j=3, so [2,3].

examples.input: n=3, k=5, iterate i from 1 to 2
examples.trace: Since k>=n, start_j is always 0. i=1: start_j=max(0,1-5)=0, end_j=0, so [0,0]. i=2: start_j=max(0,2-5)=0, end_j=1, so [0,1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Access and manipulate elements in an array using indices, including boundary checks.
ladders.explanation: An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements accessible by index starting from 0. For an array nums of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Accessing nums[i] gives the element at position i. Basic operations include reading nums[i], writing nums[i]=value, and iterating with for i in range(n): process nums[i]. Boundary checks are crucial to avoid index errors: ensure 0 ≤ i < n. In this problem, we also calculate derived indices like i-k, which may be negative, so we use max(0, i-k) to clamp to valid range. The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) access, making them efficient for DP storage. Connection to L4: This foundation is used to traverse the nums array and compute dp array.

examples.trace: nums[0] = first element = 10. nums[1] = second = 20. nums[2] = third = 30. Index 3 would be invalid (out of bounds).

examples.input: Calculate max(0, i-k) for i=2, k=3
examples.trace: i-k = 2-3 = -1. max(0, -1) = 0, since negative indices are not allowed in array access.

approaches.name: DP with Monotonic Queue O(n)
approaches.summary: Employ a monotonic decreasing deque to maintain candidate dp values within the sliding window of size k, allowing O(1) access to the maximum. This is the most optimal approach for this problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game VI with DP and Monotonic Queue
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with monotonic decreasing deque to find maximum score path with k-step jumps.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum score achievable by starting at index 0 and making jumps of at most k steps forward to reach the last index. The naive DP approach would be O(nk) by checking all possible previous positions within k steps. The key optimization is using a monotonic decreasing deque to maintain candidate dp values within the sliding window of size k, allowing O(1) access to the maximum. We maintain a deque that stores indices in decreasing order of their dp values. At each position i, we calculate dp[i] = nums[i] + dp[deque.front()] (the maximum dp value within the last k indices). Before adding i to the deque, we remove indices from the back that have dp values ≤ dp[i] to maintain decreasing order, and remove from the front indices that are outside the k-sized window. This ensures we always have the maximum dp value for the current window at the front. The intuition is that for any position j within the window, if dp[j] ≤ dp[i] and j < i, then j will never be the optimal choice for future positions since i is both better (higher dp) and more recent (will stay in window longer). Time complexity is O(n) as each index enters and exits the deque at most once. Space is O(n) for dp array and O(k) for deque.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0]=1, deque=[0]. i=1: window indices 0-0, max dp at deque[0]=0, dp[1]=nums[1]+dp[0]=-1+1=0. Remove from deque back indices with dp≤dp[1]: dp[0]=1>0, keep. Add 1 to deque: [0,1]. i=2: window indices 0-1, front=0, dp[2]=-2+1=-1. Remove from back: dp[1]=0>-1, keep. Add 2: [0,1,2]. Remove front if out of window: 0 is within k=2 (i-0=2≤k), keep. i=3: window indices 1-2, front=1 (dp=0), dp[3]=4+0=4. Remove from back: dp[2]=-1<4, remove 2; dp[1]=0<4, remove 1; dp[0]=1<4, remove 0. Add 3: [3]. i=4: window indices 2-3, front=3, dp[4]=-7+4=-3. Remove from back: dp[3]=4>-3, keep. Add 4: [3,4]. Remove front if out: 3 is within k (i-3=1≤2), keep. i=5: window indices 3-4, front=3, dp[5]=3+4=7. Final dp=[1,0,-1,4,-3,7], max score=dp[5]=7.

dp[0]=10, deque=[0]. i=1: front=0, dp[1]=-5+10=5, deque=[0,1]. i=2: front=0, dp[2]=-2+10=8, remove back: dp[1]=5<8, remove 1; dp[0]=10>8, keep. Add 2: [0,2]. i=3: window indices 0-2, front=0, dp[3]=4+10=14. Remove back: dp[2]=8<14, remove 2; dp[0]=10<14, remove 0. Add 3: [3]. i=4: front=3, dp[4]=0+14=14, deque=[3,4]. i=5: window indices 2-4, front=3, dp[5]=3+14=17. Final dp=[10,5,8,14,14,17], output=17.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Monotonic Decreasing Deque for Sliding Window Maximum
ladders.desc: Implement the deque maintenance logic that keeps maximum dp value accessible in O(1) within k-sized window.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the deque operations that maintain a monotonic decreasing sequence of indices based on their dp values, while ensuring all indices in the deque are within the current sliding window of size k. At each step when processing index i, we perform three operations: 1) Remove from the front if the index is outside the window (i - deque[0] > k). 2) The current dp[i] is calculated as nums[i] + dp[deque[0]] (if deque not empty). 3) Remove from the back while deque is not empty and dp[deque[-1]] ≤ dp[i], then append i. The intuition is that we want to keep only indices that could be the maximum for future windows. If we have two indices j < i with dp[j] ≤ dp[i], then j is useless because i has equal or better value and will remain in the window longer (since it's more recent). This guarantees the front always has the maximum dp value for the current window. The time complexity per operation is amortized O(1) as each index enters and exits deque at most once. Space is O(k) for the deque.

examples.trace: Initialize deque=[0] with dp[0]=2. i=1: Remove front if out: 0 is within k=2 (1-0=1). dp[1]=nums[1]+dp[deque[0]]=3+2=5. Remove from back: dp[0]=2≤5, remove 0. Add 1: deque=[1]. i=2: Remove front: 1 is within k (2-1=1). dp[2]=1+dp[1]=1+5=6. Remove from back: dp[1]=5>6? No (5≤6 is false), keep. Add 2: deque=[1,2]. i=3: Remove front: check 1 (3-1=2≤k), keep. dp[3]=4+dp[1]=4+5=9. Remove from back: dp[2]=6≤9, remove 2; dp[1]=5≤9, remove 1. Add 3: deque=[3]. Final dp=[2,5,6,9].

deque=[0]. i=1: Remove front: 0 is within k=1 (1-0=1). dp[1]=-2+5=3. Remove from back: dp[0]=5>3, keep. Add 1: deque=[0,1]. But need to ensure window size: i=1, window indices 0-0? Actually for k=1, valid previous indices are i-1 only. So at i=1, only index 0 is valid. deque[0]=0 is valid. After adding 1, deque=[0,1], but before i=2, we must remove front if out: for i=2, check deque[0]=0: 2-0=2>k=1, so remove 0. Then dp[2]=3+dp[deque[0]=1]=3+3=6. Wait, correct: i=1: dp[1]=3, deque becomes [0,1]. i=2: Remove front: 0 is out (2-0=2>1), remove 0, deque=[1]. dp[2]=3+dp[1]=3+3=6. Remove from back: dp[1]=3≤6, remove 1. Add 2: deque=[2]. Final dp=[5,3,6].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sliding Window Maximum with Deque
ladders.desc: Find maximum value in each sliding window of size k using a monotonic deque.
ladders.explanation: This core logic solves the problem: given an array, for each window of size k, find the maximum value. The naive approach would be O(nk). The optimized approach uses a deque (double-ended queue) to store indices of elements in decreasing order. For each new element at index i, we: 1) Remove from the front if the index is out of the current window (i - deque[0] ≥ k). 2) Remove from the back while the element at deque[-1] is ≤ the new element nums[i], to maintain decreasing order. 3) Append i to the deque. The maximum for the window ending at i is then nums[deque[0]]. The intuition is that we only need to keep elements that could be the maximum in future windows. If a smaller element comes before a larger one, it will never be the maximum once the larger element enters the window. This gives O(n) time as each index is pushed and popped at most once. Space is O(k). This is directly applicable to Jump Game VI where we need the maximum dp value in the last k indices.

examples.trace: deque = []. i=0: deque=[0]. i=1: remove back: nums[0]=1≤3, remove 0; add 1: [1]. i=2: remove back: nums[1]=3≤-1? No, keep; add 2: [1,2]. Window at i=2: indices 0-2, max=nums[deque[0]=1]=3. i=3: remove front if out: deque[0]=1, 3-1=2<k, keep. remove back: nums[2]=-1≤-3? No; add 3: [1,2,3]. Window i=3: max=nums[1]=3. i=4: remove front: deque[0]=1, 4-1=3≥k, remove 1; deque=[2,3]. remove back: nums[3]=-3≤5, remove 3; nums[2]=-1≤5, remove 2; add 4: [4]. Window i=4: max=nums[4]=5. i=5: remove front: 4 is within k (5-4=1), keep. remove back: nums[4]=5≤3? No; add 5: [4,5]. Window i=5: max=nums[4]=5. i=6: remove front: 4 is out? 6-4=2<k, keep. remove back: nums[5]=3≤6, remove 5; nums[4]=5≤6, remove 4; add 6: [6]. Window i=6: max=nums[6]=6. i=7: remove front: 6 is within k (7-6=1), keep. remove back: nums[6]=6≤7, remove 6; add 7: [7]. Window i=7: max=nums[7]=7. Output maxima: [3,3,5,5,6,7].

deque=[]. i=0: [0]. i=1: remove back: nums[0]=5≤4? No; add 1: [0,1]. Window i=1: max=nums[0]=5. i=2: remove front: 0 is out? 2-0=2≥k, remove 0; deque=[1]. remove back: nums[1]=4≤3? No; add 2: [1,2]. Window i=2: max=nums[1]=4. i=3: remove front: 1 is out? 3-1=2≥k, remove 1; deque=[2]. remove back: nums[2]=3≤2? No; add 3: [2,3]. Window i=3: max=nums[2]=3. i=4: remove front: 2 is out? 4-2=2≥k, remove 2; deque=[3]. remove back: nums[3]=2≤1? No; add 4: [3,4]. Window i=4: max=nums[3]=2. Output: [5,4,3,2].

ladders.title: Building Block — Dynamic Programming with State Transition
ladders.desc: Define dp[i] as maximum score to reach index i with jumps of at most k steps.
ladders.explanation: This building block establishes the DP formulation for the problem. Let dp[i] represent the maximum score achievable to reach index i starting from index 0, with the rule that you can jump from any previous index j where 1 ≤ i-j ≤ k. The recurrence relation is: dp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[j]) for all j in [i-k, i-1]. For i=0, dp[0] = nums[0] as starting point. The final answer is dp[n-1]. The intuition is that to reach i, you must come from some previous index j within k steps, and the score at i is nums[i] plus the best score to reach j. This is a classic DP problem similar to climbing stairs with a variable jump range. The naive implementation would iterate over all j for each i, giving O(nk) time. The challenge is optimizing the max(dp[j]) query over the sliding window of size k, which leads to the monotonic queue optimization. This DP approach is foundational because it breaks the problem into overlapping subproblems: the optimal way to reach i depends only on optimal ways to reach previous indices within k steps.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Deque Push and Pop with Index Maintenance
ladders.desc: Implement deque operations to add new indices and remove outdated ones based on window constraints.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves manipulating a double-ended queue (deque) to maintain a list of indices subject to two rules: 1) Remove indices from the front if they fall outside the current sliding window (i.e., index ≤ i-k). 2) Before adding a new index i, remove indices from the back if their corresponding values are ≤ the value for i, to keep the deque in decreasing order of values. This ensures the front always has the index of the maximum value in the window. The intuition is that we use the deque as a cache of potential maximum candidates. Removing from the front handles the sliding window constraint efficiently without scanning all k elements. Removing from the back maintains the monotonic property, which is crucial for O(1) access to the maximum. In Python, collections.deque is typically used. Time per operation is O(1) amortized. This operation is fundamental because it's the engine that drives the sliding window maximum algorithm.

examples.input: values = [5,3,8], indices in deque = [0,1] with values [5,3], new index=2 with value=8, k=2
examples.trace: Current deque: [0,1] corresponding to values [5,3]. For new index i=2 with value=8. Step 1: Remove from front if out of window: window for i=2 is indices 0 to 2 (since k=2, valid indices are i-2=0 to i-1=1). deque[0]=0 is within window (2-0=2≤k), keep. Step 2: Remove from back while value at deque[-1] ≤ new value: deque[-1]=1, value=3 ≤ 8, so remove 1. Now deque=[0]. Check again: deque[-1]=0, value=5 ≤ 8, remove 0. Deque becomes empty. Step 3: Append new index 2. Final deque=[2].

examples.input: values = [4,2,5,1], deque = [0,1] with values [4,2], new index=2 with value=5, k=1
examples.trace: Deque [0,1] values [4,2]. i=2, value=5, k=1. Step 1: Remove front if out: window for i=2 is indices 1 to 1 (since k=1, only index i-1=1 is valid). deque[0]=0 is out because 2-0=2>k=1, so remove 0. Deque becomes [1]. Step 2: Remove from back: deque[-1]=1, value=2 ≤ 5, remove 1. Deque empty. Step 3: Append 2. Final deque=[2].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sliding Window and Deque Introduction
ladders.desc: Understand sliding window technique and deque data structure for efficient range queries.
ladders.explanation: A sliding window is a subarray of fixed or variable size that moves across an array. For example, with window size k=3 moving across an array of length n, the windows are indices [0,2], [1,3], [2,4], etc. The challenge is to compute something for each window efficiently without recalculating from scratch. A deque (double-ended queue) is a data structure that allows insertion and deletion at both ends in O(1) time. It can be used to maintain a list of elements where we need to frequently access the front or back. In the context of sliding windows, a deque helps track elements that are relevant to the current window, allowing us to remove elements that slide out of the window from the front, and add new elements from the back. The intuition is that by keeping the deque sorted in a specific order (e.g., decreasing), we can quickly answer queries like 'what is the maximum in the current window?' without scanning all k elements each time. This combines two simple ideas: 1) Windows overlap, so we can reuse information. 2) Deques allow efficient maintenance of candidate elements. This foundation is crucial for optimizing problems where you need to repeatedly query a moving range.

examples.trace: Start with indices 0 to 2: elements [2,1,3] form the first window. Slide right by 1: indices 1 to 3: elements [1,3,4] form the second window. Slide right by 1: indices 2 to 4: elements [3,4,5] form the third window. For each window, if we want the maximum, naive way is to scan all 3 elements each time: max of first window=3, second=4, third=5. With deque, we can maintain decreasing order: for first window, deque stores indices [0,1,2] with values [2,1,3] but after processing, we might keep [2] only if maintaining decreasing. Actually, to get maximum, we want deque in decreasing order: after seeing 2 (index 0), deque=[0]; see 1 (index 1), since 1≤2, keep deque=[0,1]; see 3 (index 2), remove from back while ≤3: remove 1 (value 1≤3), remove 0 (value 2≤3), deque=[2]. So max for window ending at 2 is value at index 2=3.

Windows: indices 0-1: [5,4]; indices 1-2: [4,3]; indices 2-3: [3,2]. Using deque to track maximum: start with index 0 (value 5), deque=[0]. Index 1 (value 4): since 4≤5, keep deque=[0,1]. For window at i=1 (indices 0-1), max is value at deque[0]=5. Next, index 2 (value 3): first remove front if out: for window at i=2, valid indices are 1-2, so index 0 is out (2-0=2≥k), remove 0, deque=[1]. Then, since 3≤4, keep deque=[1,2]. Max for window at i=2 is value at index 1=4. Index 3 (value 2): remove front if out: index 1 is out? 3-1=2≥k, remove 1, deque=[2]. Since 2≤3, keep deque=[2,3]. Max for window at i=3 is value at index 2=3.
